sources.full_name: WHO Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality, 4th Edition incorporating 1st & 2nd Addenda
Nigerian Industrial Standard for Drinking Water Quality (NSDWQ)
sources.note: NIS 554:2015 supersedes NIS 554:2007. Limits for priority metals (Pb, As, Cd, Cr, Hg) are harmonised with WHO values.

_metadata.maintainer: Review and update this file whenever WHO or SON publishes a new edition.
_metadata.note: physical_min / physical_max are realistic measurement bounds used for input validation only -- not compliance limits. Each standard entry carries a 'standard_date' field showing when that authority issued or last revised the limit.

standards.consequence: Below 6.5: pipe corrosion and metallic taste. Above 8.5: scale buildup and reduced disinfection.
standards.solution: Neutralize with Lime (low pH) or Acid Injection (high pH).

standards.consequence: Outside 6.5-8.5: affects taste, chlorine disinfection efficiency, and pipe integrity.
standards.solution: pH adjustment using lime or CO2 injection.

standards.consequence: Interferes with disinfection; aesthetically unpleasing and indicative of contamination.
standards.solution: Coagulation, Flocculation, and Filtration.

standards.consequence: High turbidity shields bacteria from chlorine; reduces disinfection efficacy.
standards.solution: Optimise filtration. For effective chlorination turbidity must be below 1 NTU.

standards.consequence: Salty taste; scale formation in pipes and appliances.
standards.solution: Reverse Osmosis (RO).

standards.consequence: Gastrointestinal irritation; unpalatable water above 1000 mg/L.
standards.solution: Desalination or RO treatment.

standards.consequence: Elevated conductivity indicates high dissolved salts, corrosive potential.
standards.solution: Reverse Osmosis or Ion Exchange.

standards.consequence: Aesthetically unacceptable; may indicate organic matter or metal contamination.
standards.solution: Coagulation-flocculation-filtration or activated carbon treatment.

standards.consequence: Indicative of dissolved organic matter; reduces consumer confidence.
standards.solution: Coagulation and filtration. Investigate source contamination.

standards.consequence: High temperature accelerates bacterial growth and promotes pipe corrosion.
standards.solution: Cool storage; prevent sunlight exposure on water pipes and tanks.

standards.consequence: Low DO indicates organic pollution or anaerobic conditions; promotes H2S odour.
standards.solution: Aeration (cascade or diffuser aerators) to raise DO levels.

standards.consequence: DO below 4 mg/L promotes taste and odour problems and corrosion.
standards.solution: Aeration treatment.

standards.consequence: Consumer complaints; may indicate contamination.
standards.solution: Identify source; activated carbon or alternative treatment.

standards.consequence: Unpleasant taste reduces consumer confidence and may indicate contamination.
standards.solution: Activated carbon filtration; investigate source.

standards.consequence: Consumer complaints; may indicate contamination.
standards.solution: Aeration, activated carbon, or chlorination.

standards.consequence: Unpleasant odour signals possible contamination or excessive organics.
standards.solution: Aeration; activated carbon; investigate source.

standards.consequence: Taste impairment and corrosion of concrete infrastructure.
standards.solution: Use PVC pipes or apply Reverse Osmosis treatment.

standards.consequence: Laxative effect and diarrhea above 500 mg/L; aesthetic threshold is 250 mg/L.
standards.solution: Reverse Osmosis or Ion Exchange.

standards.consequence: Reddish-brown staining of laundry and fixtures; metallic taste.
standards.solution: Aeration followed by sand filtration.

standards.consequence: Aesthetic nuisance (staining, taste). No formal health-based limit set.
standards.solution: Oxidation and filtration.

standards.consequence: CRITICAL: Neurotoxin causing irreversible brain damage especially in children. Kidney damage, hypertension. No safe level.
standards.solution: Replace lead service pipes. Maintain pH > 7.5 to reduce leaching. Coagulation with alum or RO.

standards.consequence: Severe neurotoxicity, especially in children. Developmental delays, reduced IQ, behavioral problems. Accumulates in bones and soft tissues.
standards.solution: pH control (7.5-8.0), orthophosphate corrosion inhibitors, replace lead pipes. RO if source contamination.

standards.consequence: Blue Baby Syndrome (Methemoglobinemia) in infants under 6 months. Indicator of agricultural runoff or sewage.
standards.solution: Ion Exchange or Reverse Osmosis. DO NOT boil - boiling concentrates nitrates. Control fertilizer/sewage runoff.

standards.consequence: Methemoglobinemia ('blue baby syndrome') in infants. Nitrate reduces oxygen-carrying capacity of blood.
standards.solution: Biological denitrification, Ion Exchange, or RO. Source protection: control agricultural runoff.

standards.consequence: Dental fluorosis (brown/mottled teeth) above 1.5 mg/L. Skeletal fluorosis above 4 mg/L.
standards.solution: Activated Alumina (Nalgonda Technique) or Bone Char filtration. Reverse Osmosis.

standards.consequence: Dental fluorosis (cosmetic enamel defects) and skeletal fluorosis (bone/joint pain) at high levels. Essential trace element at low doses.
standards.solution: Bone char or Activated Alumina adsorption. Contact precipitation with lime and alum. RO. Climate and water consumption should be considered when setting national limits.

standards.consequence: Group 1 Carcinogen. Causes skin, lung, bladder cancer and skin lesions.
standards.solution: Activated Alumina adsorption or Coagulation with iron salts.

standards.consequence: Chronic exposure causes arsenicosis: skin lesions, hyperkeratosis, cancer of skin, lungs, bladder, and kidney.
standards.solution: Coagulation-flocculation with ferric chloride. Activated Alumina. RO. Lime softening. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Accumulates in kidneys causing renal tubular damage (Itai-itai disease). Carcinogen.
standards.solution: Coagulation-flocculation, lime softening, or RO. Remove galvanized pipe sources.

standards.consequence: Renal tubular dysfunction and bone demineralization. Accumulates in kidney and liver. Probable carcinogen.
standards.solution: Coagulation with ferric salts. Lime softening. Ion Exchange. RO.

standards.consequence: Hexavalent Cr is a known carcinogen. Causes skin sensitisation, lung cancer, liver and kidney damage.
standards.solution: Coagulation at controlled pH, reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) with ferrous sulphate, then precipitation.

standards.consequence: Cr(VI) is carcinogenic; Cr(III) is essential trace element. Limit applies to total chromium.
standards.solution: Reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) using ferrous sulfate, then coagulation. Ion Exchange. RO.

standards.consequence: Gastrointestinal distress (nausea/vomiting) and liver damage at high concentrations.
standards.solution: pH adjustment to reduce pipe corrosivity (keep pH > 7.0). Pipe replacement or Ion Exchange.

standards.consequence: Acute GI disturbance (nausea, vomiting) at levels >3 mg/L. Staining of laundry and sanitary ware may occur below guideline value.
standards.solution: Corrosion control: pH adjustment (7.5-8.0), orthophosphate inhibitors. Replace copper pipes if leaching severe.

standards.consequence: CRITICAL: Powerful neurotoxin. Causes Minamata disease, kidney failure, neurological damage.
standards.solution: Activated carbon adsorption, coagulation, or RO. Source elimination is critical.

standards.consequence: Inorganic mercury: renal toxicity and neurological effects. Chronic exposure causes tremor, gingivitis, psychological disturbances.
standards.solution: Coagulation-flocculation. Activated carbon. Lime softening. RO. Eliminate industrial sources.

standards.consequence: Causes allergic skin reactions; possible carcinogen with chronic exposure.
standards.solution: Ion Exchange or RO. Check stainless steel fittings for leaching.

standards.consequence: Allergic contact dermatitis in sensitized individuals. Chronic oral exposure may affect body weight and organ function.
standards.solution: Ion Exchange. Lime softening. RO. Investigate source (industrial discharge or pipe materials).

standards.consequence: Astringent (greasy) taste and milky appearance above 3 mg/L.
standards.solution: Ion Exchange or flushing of galvanized pipes.

standards.consequence: Aesthetic issue: astringent taste and opalescence. No formal health-based limit; essential trace element.
standards.solution: Pipe flushing. Replace corroded galvanized pipes. Ion Exchange if groundwater source.

standards.consequence: Black stains on laundry and plumbing; neurological effects with chronic exposure.
standards.solution: Chemical oxidation with potassium permanganate or chlorine, then filtration. Greensand filtration.

standards.consequence: Neurological effects similar to Parkinson's disease at high chronic exposure. Aesthetic issues (staining, taste) at >0.1 mg/L.
standards.solution: Oxidation (KMnO4, Cl2, O3) followed by filtration. Greensand or pyrolusite filtration. RO.

standards.consequence: Cardiovascular effects; increases blood pressure and causes kidney damage.
standards.solution: Ion Exchange, lime softening, or RO.

standards.consequence: Elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular effects. Barium sulfate (insoluble) is non-toxic; soluble barium salts are toxic.
standards.solution: Lime softening (converts to insoluble BaSO4). Ion Exchange. RO.

standards.consequence: Direct cause of methemoglobinemia. More toxic than nitrate. Indicates recent organic contamination.
standards.solution: Aeration, chlorination, and Ion Exchange. Investigate sewage infiltration. Biological nitrification filter.

standards.consequence: Methemoglobinemia in infants. Nitrite is more potent than nitrate in causing blue baby syndrome. Indicates recent fecal or organic pollution.
standards.solution: Chlorination (oxidizes nitrite to nitrate). Investigate sewage leaks. Biological treatment.

standards.consequence: Scale formation in pipes, boilers, and appliances; high soap consumption.
standards.solution: Water Softening via Lime-Soda process or Ion Exchange.

standards.consequence: No formal health-based limit. Aesthetic: scale deposition above 200 mg/L; increased soap usage. Hard water may reduce cardiac disease risk.
standards.solution: Lime-soda softening. Ion Exchange (sodium cycle). Reverse Osmosis.

standards.consequence: Hair and nail loss (selenosis); liver and nervous system damage.
standards.solution: RO, coagulation at low pH, or Activated Alumina adsorption.

standards.consequence: Chronic excess causes selenosis: hair/nail loss, neurological effects. Essential trace element but toxic above limits.
standards.solution: Reverse Osmosis or Activated Alumina adsorption.

standards.consequence: Inhibits cellular oxygen utilisation. Even low doses cause chronic neurological effects.
standards.solution: Chlorination at high pH (alkaline chlorination) or activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Acute poisoning at high levels. Chronic exposure causes thyroid and neurological damage.
standards.solution: Alkaline chlorination, ozone oxidation, or activated carbon adsorption.

standards.consequence: Salty taste above 250 mg/L. Corrosive to metal pipes and infrastructure.
standards.solution: RO or electrodialysis for high-chloride groundwater.

standards.consequence: No formal health-based limit. Taste threshold ~250 mg/L. Corrosive to distribution systems.
standards.solution: RO treatment. Investigate source (seawater intrusion or industrial discharge).

standards.consequence: Pungent odour; indicator of sewage or agricultural contamination. Reacts with chlorine reducing disinfection.
standards.solution: Breakpoint chlorination or biological nitrification filter. Investigate contamination source.

standards.consequence: No formal health-based guideline. Aesthetic limit of 1.5 mg/L based on odour threshold. Reduces chlorine disinfection efficiency.
standards.solution: Aeration or biological nitrification. Reduce at source.

standards.consequence: Below 0.2 mg/L: insufficient disinfection residual in distribution. Above 0.5 mg/L: taste/odour complaints and DBP risk.
standards.solution: Adjust chlorine dose at treatment plant. Check contact time (min 30 min at pH < 8.0).

standards.consequence: Below 0.5 mg/L after 30 min contact: inadequate disinfection. Above 5 mg/L: taste complaints and disinfection by-products (THMs).
standards.solution: Maintain minimum 0.5 mg/L free chlorine after 30 min contact at pH < 8.0. Maintain residual throughout distribution.

standards.consequence: Reproductive toxicity and developmental effects at high doses.
standards.solution: RO (partial removal only). Ion Exchange with boron-selective resins.

standards.consequence: Disinfection by-product of chlorination. Known carcinogens (chloroform, bromoform). Linked to bladder cancer.
standards.solution: Reduce organic precursors before chlorination. Use alternative disinfectants (ozone, UV). Activate carbon post-treatment.

standards.consequence: Chloroform: possible carcinogen. Chronic exposure linked to liver, kidney, and bladder cancer.
standards.solution: Remove Natural Organic Matter (NOM) before disinfection. Consider ozonation or UV.

standards.consequence: Potential neurodegenerative disorders with chronic exposure.
standards.solution: Optimize alum dosing if used as coagulant; consider alternative coagulants.

standards.consequence: Aesthetic/operational parameter. No confirmed health effects in drinking water. May cause precipitation and consumer complaints.
standards.solution: Optimize coagulation pH and dose. Use alternative coagulants (ferric salts).

standards.consequence: Salty taste at high levels; concern for individuals on sodium-restricted diets.
standards.solution: Reverse osmosis for high-sodium groundwater.

standards.consequence: Taste threshold ~200 mg/L. Concern for individuals with hypertension or on sodium-restricted diets. No formal health-based limit.
standards.solution: RO treatment. Electrodialysis.

standards.consequence: Contributes to hardness; laxative effect at very high levels.
standards.solution: Water softening; lime-soda or ion exchange process.

standards.consequence: No formal health-based limit. Contributes to hardness and taste. Laxative effect above 700 mg/L (as MgSO4).
standards.solution: Lime softening. Ion Exchange.

standards.consequence: Rotten egg odour; indicator of anaerobic conditions.
standards.solution: Aeration, chlorination, or activated carbon filtration.

standards.consequence: Aesthetic issue: rotten egg odour at very low concentrations (~0.05 mg/L). Promotes corrosion.
standards.solution: Aeration. Chlorination (oxidizes to sulfate). Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Gastrointestinal effects at high doses. Potential carcinogen.
standards.solution: Coagulation-flocculation. RO. Activated alumina.

standards.consequence: Renal toxicity (chemical, not radiological). Accumulates in kidney causing tubular damage.
standards.solution: Ion Exchange. Lime softening. RO. Coagulation.

standards.consequence: Taste and odour when chlorinated; possibly carcinogenic.
standards.solution: Activated carbon; ozonation.

standards.consequence: Foaming; possible carcinogen.
standards.solution: Activated carbon adsorption; advanced oxidation processes.

standards.consequence: Taste and odour; possible carcinogen.
standards.solution: Activated carbon; oil separation at source.

standards.consequence: Toxic and possibly carcinogenic depending on type.
standards.solution: Activated carbon; source protection and watershed management.

standards.consequence: Known carcinogens.
standards.solution: Activated carbon; source control.

standards.consequence: Benzo[a]pyrene is representative PAH. Carcinogenic.
standards.solution: Activated carbon. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Indicator of organic contamination; precursor to disinfection by-products.
standards.solution: Enhanced coagulation; activated carbon; ozonation before chlorination.

standards.consequence: Chlorination by-product; carcinogenic.
standards.solution: Remove organic precursors before disinfection. Consider ozonation or UV.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic DBP. Taste/odour issues.
standards.solution: Reduce NOM before chlorination. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Known carcinogen (leukemia). Volatile organic compound.
standards.solution: Aeration (air stripping). Activated carbon. Eliminate source.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic (liver cancer). From PVC pipe degradation.
standards.solution: Aeration. Replace degraded PVC pipes. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Neurological effects. Taste/odour at lower levels.
standards.solution: Aeration (air stripping). Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Neurological effects. Taste/odour concern.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Neurological effects. Taste/odour concern.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Hepatotoxic and carcinogenic.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic. Liver/kidney effects.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic. Dry cleaning solvent.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Liver/kidney effects. Taste/odour concern.
standards.solution: Aeration. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic THM. Liver effects.
standards.solution: Reduce NOM before chlorination. Activated carbon.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic THM.
standards.solution: Reduce NOM and bromide before chlorination.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic THM.
standards.solution: Reduce NOM before chlorination.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic THM.
standards.solution: Reduce NOM before chlorination.

standards.consequence: Hemolytic effects. DBP from chlorine dioxide.
standards.solution: Optimize ClO2 dose. Reduce contact time.

standards.consequence: Hemolytic effects. From hypochlorite degradation.
standards.solution: Use fresh hypochlorite. Proper storage.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic. DBP from ozonation.
standards.solution: Reduce bromide in source. Optimize ozone dose. Use UV or H2O2.

standards.consequence: Alternative disinfectant. Less reactive than chlorine.
standards.solution: Maintain appropriate residual for disinfection.

standards.consequence: Herbicide. Hormonal effects. Includes metabolites.
standards.solution: Activated carbon. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic herbicide.
standards.solution: Activated carbon. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Neurological and developmental effects. Insecticide.
standards.solution: Activated carbon. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Carcinogenic. Persistent organochlorine pesticide.
standards.solution: Activated carbon. Source elimination (banned in most countries).

standards.consequence: Kidney/liver effects. Herbicide (2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid).
standards.solution: Activated carbon. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Increases cancer risk with chronic exposure.
standards.solution: Source protection; reverse osmosis; ion exchange; lime softening.

standards.consequence: Screening level. Investigate if exceeded. Cancer risk.
standards.solution: RO. Ion exchange. Lime softening.

standards.consequence: Hepatotoxic cyanotoxin. From cyanobacteria blooms. Total (free + cell-bound).
standards.solution: Source protection (reduce nutrients). Powdered activated carbon. Oxidation (ozone, permanganate).

standards.consequence: Hepatotoxic cyanotoxin. From cyanobacteria. NEW in WHO 2022.
standards.solution: Source protection. Powdered activated carbon. Ozonation.

standards.consequence: Neurotoxic cyanotoxin. Acute exposure. From cyanobacteria. NEW in WHO 2022.
standards.solution: Source protection. Powdered activated carbon. Oxidation.

standards.consequence: FECAL CONTAMINATION CONFIRMED: Immediate Cholera, Typhoid, and dysentery risk.
standards.solution: Immediate Disinfection with Chlorine (boil water advisory). Investigate source of contamination.

standards.consequence: FECAL CONTAMINATION: Direct indicator of pathogenic risk. Water pathogenically unsafe.
standards.solution: Emergency chlorination. Do not distribute water. Inspect entire system integrity.

standards.consequence: Indicates environmental or fecal contamination of the distribution system.
standards.solution: Check system integrity for pipe cracks. Shock-chlorinate distribution system.

standards.consequence: Should be absent in treated piped water. Presence indicates treatment failure.
standards.solution: Disinfection. Audit treatment process (filtration + chlorination). Inspect network for intrusion.

standards.consequence: High HPC indicates general microbial activity and possible biofilm growth in distribution pipes.
standards.solution: Improve chlorine residual in distribution. Clean and flush pipe network.

standards.consequence: Indicator of treatment efficiency and biological stability. Not directly pathogenic but signals system issues.
standards.solution: Maintain adequate chlorine residual. Investigate stagnation points in distribution.

standards.consequence: Gastrointestinal illness; resistant to chlorine disinfection.
standards.solution: Multi-barrier approach: source water protection, adequate treatment including filtration, disinfection, and well-maintained distribution system.

standards.consequence: Protozoan parasite. Causes giardiasis (diarrhea). Very resistant to chlorine.
standards.solution: Filtration (especially slow sand). UV disinfection. Ozonation. Source protection.

standards.consequence: Severe diarrhea; highly resistant to chlorine; dangerous for immunocompromised.
standards.solution: Multi-barrier approach: source water protection, filtration (especially slow sand or membrane), UV disinfection.

standards.consequence: Extremely resistant to chlorine. Severe gastroenteritis. Life-threatening for immunocompromised.
standards.solution: Membrane filtration. UV disinfection (high dose). Ozonation. Chlorine ineffective.

standards.consequence: Indicator of recent fecal contamination.
standards.solution: Disinfection; investigate contamination source; repair system integrity.

standards.consequence: Alternative fecal indicator. More resistant to disinfection than E. coli.
standards.solution: Disinfection. Investigate source. System repair.

standards.consequence: Index of intermittent fecal contamination; spores resistant to disinfection.
standards.solution: Improve filtration; investigate intermittent contamination events; disinfection.

standards.consequence: Indicator of treatment effectiveness. Spore-forming; very resistant to disinfection and environmental stress.
standards.solution: Improve filtration. Investigate source protection failures.

standards.consequence: Scale formation in pipes, boilers, and appliances; high soap consumption. Aesthetic concern.
standards.solution: Water Softening via Lime-Soda process or Ion Exchange.

standards.consequence: No formal health-based limit. Aesthetic: scale deposition above 200 mg/L. Hard water may reduce cardiac disease risk.
standards.solution: Lime-soda softening. Ion Exchange. RO if needed.
